title: A videogame for perceived risk of harm from opioid misuse in adolescents: a randomized controlled trial

data_scope.design: two-arm parallel superiority unblinded RCT with placebo comparator
data_scope.primary_outcome: perceived great risk of harm from opioid misuse at 3 months

core_conclusion: PlaySmart, a theory-driven videogame, did not significantly raise adolescents' perceived great risk of harm from opioid misuse at the primary 3-month endpoint (29% vs 23%, P=0.14). It produced a transient risk-perception gain at 6 weeks (P=0.047) and durable improvements in opioid knowledge (P<0.001) and negative expectancies (P=0.001), with high engagement; the risk-perception effect decayed by 3 months, suggesting a need for booster content.
why_it_matters: Rare placebo-controlled RCT of a serious game targeting a lethal, rising harm (adolescent opioid misuse); an informative null with a 6-week signal that localizes when and where digital prevention works, and a scalable delivery channel that bypasses school-program staffing barriers.
why_nature: High population-health relevance to the opioid epidemic; pre-registered intention-to-treat RCT with rigorous mixed-effects analysis; transparent reporting of a null primary outcome with nuanced secondary signals; digital-health scalability framing of broad interest.
added_value_to_my_research: Geocode the 15 schools into a spatial efficacy surface via 2SFCA/R2SFCA and MGWR
Memory-decay/survival model of risk-perception decay -> optimal booster-timing optimizer
Learned personalized content-sequencing policy with explainable, equity-audited decisions
Fuse intervention efficacy with environmental exposome (urbanicity, greenspace, deprivation)
Digital-divide / telehealth-equity audit of national scalability before dissemination
